posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked:
Going back to the 80s and 90s, California made a few nice chardonnays in Napa, some pretty mediocre Sav Blancs and a few interesting Rieslings and other varietals, but to me there was nothing exciting about white wines from California. Some of my favorites included Stony Hill, Navarro and even the occasional Montelena.

Over the last several years, however, there seems to be a bourgeoning movement of producers who are either specializing in white wine or taking it far more seriously. And moreover they are moving beyond the typical varietals to explore Rhone and Italian white varieties. My current set of favorite white wines include:

Massican – From his exploration of Italian varietals to the more traditional Chardonnay and Sauv Blanc, this is probably the most exciting winery for whites in California for my palate.

Tablas Creek – their Esprit de Tablas and Grenache Blanc are outstanding and great values (yes I know they have been around forever by California standards).

Rhys – I do think they have taken Chardonnay to a new level in California. One cannot dismiss Marcassin’s long-term track record, but the Rhys style fits my palate well.

Cattleya – she is producing some wonderful wines across the board, but I am really enjoying her Chardonnays and even her lower end Sav Blanc.

Sandlands Chenin Blanc – these wines are crisp and refreshing and are a great QPR.

I’m really looking forward to more wineries placing a premium on producing interesting white wines, especially outside of the spectrum of Chardonnay and Sav Blanc, not that there is anything wrong with those varietals.

posts.name: Adam Frisch

posts.name: Marc Hauser
posts.cooked:
Jon Bonne’s 2013 “The New California Wine” book had a whole chapter on the new wave of CA whites, in the same vein.

Massican f’in rocks across the board.

I’d also include Matthiasson’s White Wine blend as one of the truly outstanding whites.

Lots of interesting Rhône whites from Mendo and the foothills. Newfound’s Semillon comes to mind.

I may be at the point of thinking that the US does Chard better than anything else.

Wow! That’s a strong vote of confidence. The Burgundian fans will line up to keelhaul you soon.

Note that I said anything else – not anywhere else. In other words, there’s an argument to be made that the U.S. does Chard better than the U.S. does any other grape (I would put Zin as a strong contender, although I’m not generally a huge Zin fan). It’s still my view that CA/OR Chard does not yet compete with the top White Burgs. Not close. But it’s not at all out of the realm of possibility in the future. I often reach for Kutch/Walter Scott/etc over good villages Burgs. And I really couldn’t say that about any other grape.

Ultimately irrelevant to the original point, but an interesting side discussion. At the highest and broadest levels I would agree that I would take White Burgundy over any of their counterparts. That being said, I have found far more enjoyment from Massican whites than almost any $30 White Burgundy. The Meursaults and Chassagnes I used to buy for $40 to $50 are now $80 to $90 and don’t really provide that much value any more. Great wines, for sure, but it is nice to find alternatives elsewhere at a more reasonable price.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked:
I still think the best Chardonnays in CA come from David Ramey and Ramey Cellars. Aubert is up there too.

Ramey still sets the bar for US Chardonnay IMO. The mentions of Massican, Matthiasson, Ceritas, Kutch, etc. also ring true.

Unfortunately there will always be this nebulous distinction of great, world class or better than on a completely subjective assessment. I happen to find most White Burgundy (save Chablis) wildly overrated for its price level, but clearly some others would disagree.

So of course California is making great white wine. It’s just not for everyone’s taste. Same goes for every wine region.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Absolutely true. A young Ramey or Aubert, not to mention Rhys chardonnay is pretty damn good. Would be fun to do another Judgment of Paris today with some GC Burgs lined up against the best of California. Who is up to the challenge?

Count me in, but it would be very easy to tell which one was the Aubert (I have done this)…

Is that mostly because of new oak percentage, or fruit characteristics?

Side by side, they taste fatter, richer, more alcoholic. Analytically, a lot of GC white Burgundies have similar alcohols and see just as much new oak. But, side by side, my experience has been that it isn’t hard to tell which is which. I try to do this sort of thing from time to time as it “keeps one honest”, but it has been a few years, perhaps it’s time to do it again.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Don Cornwell has put Ramey wines into blind tastings of older white Burgundies and they have done very well.
It seems to me that Aubert wines would be picked out more easily as California.

As I recall Peter Watson Graff–Dick’s brother-made the '80 and '81 Chalones and those wines have held up well. Michael Michaud replaced him and his wines have also held up well.
Twenty years later Dan Karlsen, one of the funnier and smarter people on the planet, followed and his philosophy was to make wines for immediate drinking. Then Diageo bought the place…

There was a repeat of the Paris tasting ten years ago or so. Ridge and Mayacamas triumphed in the Cab part. With Chardonnay they used newer wines and Talley did well as I recall.
Talley is a winery that doesn’t get much play here but makes terrific whites every year. Au Bon Climat buys grapes from them and those wines are quite drinkable.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Absolutely true. A young Ramey or Aubert, not to mention Rhys chardonnay is pretty damn good. Would be fun to do another Judgment of Paris today with some GC Burgs lined up against the best of California. Who is up to the challenge?

Come on now, let’s line them up with a neutral party overseeing the lot and have a go at it. Doubt the Burgundians have much to lose (except some pride). It would be another story to compare these on value, as I doubt the Montrachet and clans will taste 10x better that some of those American upstarts.

True, but then they probably had less experience tasting American wines than me… I have worked harvest in California, make wine in California, and reviewed Californian wines professionally for three years. To be clear, I’m just saying it would be easy to pick out Aubert (or Marcassin, Ferren, Kistler). There are plenty of California Chardonnays that would be easy to confuse with Burgundy blind: things such as Sandhi and Rhys at the racier end of the spectrum, all the way to Ramey and perhaps even Kongsgaard at the richer end of the spectrum. It’s not just about power and weight but also about style and flavor profile.

I actually once did a tasting such as this where results were properly moderated, and 1995 Kalin Cellars CH and 2002 DRC Montrachet came out joint first…

As for pricing, that’s an interesting one. Ex-domaine, Ramonet Bâtard is cheaper than Aubert Sugar Shack, and Niellon’s Chevalier is cheaper than Marcassin. So comparing allocation pricing with allocation pricing (as opposed to retail), the white Burgundies are arguably better value.

link_counts.title: Most interesting White Wine in the world…

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
I may be at the point of thinking that the US does Chard better than anything else.

Wow! That’s a strong vote of confidence. The Burgundian fans will line up to keelhaul you soon.



Please try Ceritas Zephyr - I think it’s world class, if you like the leaner styles.

That is really the critical difference. In the entire spectrum of Chardonnay styles, Burgundian Chardonnay is principally at one end of the spectrum. They are very good that end of the spectrum, but they stay at that end. In contrast, in California you can get excellent examples from a much broader range of styles. I do not like California Chardonnay is with pHs below 2.0 because I intend to keep my teeth until I die. Other people like that type of Chardonnay and more power to them. On the other hand, there are a lot of people who like the oft-ridiculed Rombauer Chardonnay, which is the extreme at the other end of the spectrum. California Chardonnay gives you the opportunity to select the style you prefer, while Burgundian Chardonnay, albeit outstanding in many cases, tells you what style of Chardonnay to like.

As to other grapes, California makes an excellent version of some grapes, but not of others. Not a big deal, because that is true everywhere else in the world. You can’t get good Cabernet Sauvignon from The Rhine or Mosel. I wouldn’t say you can’t get good Riesling from California, because I haven’t tasted them all, but all those I have tried, are quite awful. I have certainly had plenty of excellent white Rhône varietals from California, including some excellent Viognier and Roussane.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In the entire spectrum of Chardonnay styles, Burgundian Chardonnay is principally at one end of the spectrum. They are very good that end of the spectrum, but they stay at that end.

Between things such as Domaine de la Bongran from Viré-Clessé at always >14% alcohol, with botrytis and a few grams of RS, to the raciest wines of Chablis—via the ample, enveloping grands crus of Chassagne and Pulingy and the steelier wines of Saint-Aubin or Saint-Romain—I would say that Burgundian Chardonnay spans just as big a gap as the continuum that begins with Ceritas and ends with Marcassin.

posts.name: Thomas Keim
posts.cooked:
I am certainly fond of many of the wines mentioned, but I think there were excellent Calif whites before 2005.

+1

Indeed, one could go so far as to say something like the 1980 Chalone Reserve Chardonnay from a cold cellar would be more competitive with a great white Burgundy of the same era than any of the excellent California whites being made today.

'Zackly. California’s always made great Chardonnay - The old Fisher bottlings, Duane Cronin, Kistler, David Bruce, I could go on and on. And I love the big, lusty Chardonnays, and have never been fond of people trying to emulate White Burgundy. Be thankful you have the California sun and weather and take full advantage of. Lots of great, lean Chardonnays made in Europe and our East Coast.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
In the entire spectrum of Chardonnay styles, Burgundian Chardonnay is principally at one end of the spectrum. They are very good that end of the spectrum, but they stay at that end.

Between things such as Domaine de la Bongran from Viré-Clessé at always >14% alcohol, with botrytis and a few grams of RS, to the raciest wines of Chablis—via the ample, enveloping grands crus of Chassagne and Pulingy and the steelier wines of Saint-Aubin or Saint-Romain—I would say that Burgundian Chardonnay spans just as big a gap as the continuum that begins with Ceritas and ends with Marcassin.

Unless you come to the US often, you do not have any chance to understand the breadth of wine styles in California because they are just not available on the other side of the pond. Botrytis and a few grams of RS are not the defining terms of one side of the spectrum.

You ll notice Wm is not denying he is a vampire … the only other explanation is that he is two or three people !

I was at that restaurant last month, Mel! Fortunately, they went easy on the garlic…

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked: There are some amazing things in that wine list.

posts.name: Merrill Lindquist
posts.cooked:
Then of course there is Stony Hill Chardonnay.

I have been sitting on a magnum of 1994 Stony Hill Chardonnay for quite awhile. Think there is anything in there?

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
We were talking earlier about prices, allocations, etc. Aubert, Marcassin and several other wineries allocate their wines to a mailing list. You could say the same is true for certain domaines in Burgundy. I have friends who have been buying Coche for decades. They have yet to give me the secret handshake. The local distributor for Leflaive always wanted you to buy an array of their wines, not just Leflaive or Dujac.

I suppose most of us are ITB and we have our connections, but what about the civilians amongst us?

The two wines that anybody in the USA and the UK can buy easily are ABC and Ramey. If it’s legal to ship to your state, they can do it. Berry Bros can take care of you in the UK. ABC also does well in Germany and Japan.

I see Mt Eden around, don’t know how they do overseas. I tried to get Jasper Morris MW interested in the wine for his old firm but he resisted.

The whites of TablasCreek are a good place to start.
Tom

I agree that there are some really good California white wines. But I can’t think of a single one that is superior to the best of its European counterparts.

Neither can I.

Come on folks. I’m waiting for examples other than Chardonnay.
In a thread jokingly titled "Best Hondarrabi Zuri " someone, also jokingly, said Kendall Jackson. If they had said Arnot-Roberts, I may have thought the response was serious.
I have to agree though, I can’t think of any that matches the old world counterpart, even giving latitude for the obvious differences due to climate and soil.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
What a nice coincidence. I’ll be busy Monday, but will have two Chardonnays blind at lunch, will post if I’m still compos.

I am a huge fan of California Chardonnay, especially the leaner, old-school folks. I would still say that Cabernet Sauvignon is California’s best grape.

Chardonnays, obviously not all great in every vintage, but…

Montelena
Grgich
Stony Hill
Mayacamas
Robert Mondavi (please don’t laugh; a magnum of 1985 about 5 years ago was incredible, but probably my only bottle in the last 10 years)
Stony Hill!!!
Chalone

It’s too tired and I’m too late for more, except:


SANTA CRUZ MOUNTAINS!
Mount Eden and Rhys have been mentioned.
Is Ridge Monte Bello consistently the best wine produced by Ridge? Of course I’m talking about the Chardonnay . No flies on the Santa Cruz Mountain bottling either.
My friend Barry Jackson used to make bubbly from Trout Gulch Chardonnay. I guess the grapes are a little pricey now.

Fogarty is really getting there, and there are many, many other excellent Santa Cruz Mountain Chardonnays that are not world class wines, but wonderful values.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
And, of course, earlier Marcassin. The 1996 Marcassin Hudson E Block Chard might still reign as the best CaliChard I’ve ever had and probably is in the top 30 all-time white wines.

The 1996 Lorenzo is even better IMO! Think their 1996s were better a few years ago but still holding pretty well.

that’s high praise for sure. I poured mine 4 years ago blind to a group of pretty knowledgeable people. Not one of 'em guessed California. We had some good white and red that evening and it was the unanimous WOTN, absolutely a point.

Merrill—you have to say, David zinged you pretty nicely there

I’ll try and dig for any older Stony Hill Chard notes I may have—I would guess, though, that it should show just fine if you were to open it today…mmm, yes, tried a 97 about 4 years ago as well, it was a little past, but the group tasting it felt that this was because of bottle flaw not natural causes.

I love virtually everything Kalin.

Dan–Jamie Kutch pulls from Trout Gulch to make his Santa Cruz AVA Chard. His 2014, from Trout and Zayante, gives the Marcassin I talked about some fair competition (at least for my preferred tastes). I happily have 4 more of those.

I have to agree though, I can’t think of any that matches the old world counterpart, even giving latitude for the obvious differences due to climate and soil.

Massican is making Italian variety whites that challenge the originals, and win.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
About fifteen years ago Uvaggio --my brand–and Vita Nova/il Podere --JimClendenen’s Cal Ital brands–staged a blind tasting against equally priced Marco di G selections–
The California wines triumphed at every level. I remember Raj came…also perhaps Larry Stone and another MS who did all the hard work, Sara Floyd.

Jancis compared our Vermentino to an Italian wine costing twice as much. That was over ten years ago.

They say the market makes the wine. The market seems to be opening up to Rhonish white blends and Italian styled whites.

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked: I’m kinda clueless when it comes to Chardonnay, but the whole reason I signed up with Failla was to get their concrete-finished Chardonnay Big Egg limited edition - that wine blew my mind when I tried it (it was the first time I truly loved a Chardonnay). To the point where I went, “Failla, why bother with the other Chardonnay you guys make - just make this!”. But I suppose the oaky style are much bigger sellers than the lean ones.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I agree that there are some really good California white wines. But I can’t think of a single one that is superior to the best of its European counterparts.

Neither can I.

Come on folks. I’m waiting for examples other than Chardonnay.
In a thread jokingly titled "Best Hondarrabi Zuri " someone, also jokingly, said Kendall Jackson. If they had said Arnot-Roberts, I may have thought the response was serious.
I have to agree though, I can’t think of any that matches the old world counterpart, even giving latitude for the obvious differences due to climate and soil.

There’s a huge range of soil and climate here. Real and pretend cool climates, great wines for very warm sites, etc. There’s also no shortage of European grapes that haven’t seen their potential, or are just recently finding it or getting close over the last couple decades. There are great southern European grapes that do very well in hot climates (acid retention, non-crazy sugars…) that are showing their potential, but haven’t broken out of their tiny regions. I’d argue first that there have been great CA whites for a very long time. A lot of those folks really did their research for where to plant and what winemaking techniques to employ.

On the other hand, we have the fad aspect of what grapes have market recognition and demand, and all sorts of compromises. If the market wants oaky buttery Chards that can be made with grapes from anywhere, if critics punish a steely Chard that needs age, if tasting room sales reward ready to drink, how much market space and incentive is there to really strive for the best?

The big winemaking mistakes are trying to make wine from one grape you like do another, and not understanding a site. It takes research and experimentation to get close, then you start fine tuning with a growing intimate understanding of the varied details of your site and so forth. Anyway, there’s quite a long list of white grapes currently available in CA that could be easily made to a better wine than their European counterparts. But, that’s muchly because the European ones (if there are any single variety wines made from those grapes in the first place) have yet to come close to their potential.

I taste very broadly, but not comprehensively. Like we’re seeing in off-the-beaten-path areas of Italy and other countries in Europe, we have small producers here who can push the limits with their idealism to produce small quantities, avoiding the punishing market realities of trying to move larger volumes of some unknown grape regardless of quality. Focused Picpoul fruit with structure that screams Grand Cru Chablis, anyone?

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked:
Well… I would say that Calif makes plenty of great/world-class white wines. Many are superior to their European counter-parts.
The whites of TablasCreek are a good place to start.
Tom

I agree that there are some really good California white wines. But I can’t think of a single one that is superior to the best of its European counterparts.

I could drink a really good white wine from Europe every night of the year and not repeat. Probably multiple years. I doubt I could do that from California for more than a month or two.

How about Zinfandel? I much prefer it to Primitivo, and I’m biased.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: The older Chalone Chardonnays and Pinot Blancs were amazing. Apparently the Pinot Blancs were in fact Melon, but they were outstanding white wines.

There are three different Pinot Blancs at chalone, only one of which is certified PB. The oldest is something Michaud called orange Chardonnay and then there s the melon de Bourgogne.
